Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd tell myself I was going to study for a few hours, but then I'd just end up scrolling through the LMS, watching some videos, maybe chatting with classmates online about random stuff. I logged a lot of hours but I don't think I was actually absorbing much. It felt like I was going through the motions without really having a plan.
Q2: My pattern was super inconsistent. Some weeks I'd be on the LMS constantly, especially when I felt anxious about falling behind, but then other weeks I'd barely touch anything until a deadline was breathing down my neck. I never really established a proper schedule - it was more like reactive studying based on panic rather than planning.
Q3: Usually it was anxiety that got me to log in. I'd feel this wave of guilt or worry that I was missing something important, so I'd open the LMS just to check. Sometimes I'd see notifications about discussions or new materials and that would pull me in. Other times I logged in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I wasn't really accomplishing much.
Q4: There was a brief period where I made it a habit to check the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt good for like two weeks, almost automatic. But then I had a really stressful week with some personal stuff and that routine just fell apart completely. After that, I never really got back into a consistent pattern - it became more sporadic again.
Q5: The videos were honestly my lifeline because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them at my own pace. I'd rewind parts I didn't understand and pause to take notes. The discussion forums were also surprisingly helpful because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one confused. Those peer interactions kept me somewhat connected to the course.
Q6: I pretty much ignored most of the reading materials and the longer text-based resources. My brain just couldn't focus on walls of text, especially when I was already feeling overwhelmed. I'd open them, skim the first paragraph, and then close them telling myself I'd come back later. I rarely did. The assignments themselves I kept putting off until it was basically too late.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it helped reduce my anxiety about the course. Like, watching a video that explained a concept clearly made me feel accomplished. But if something felt like it would take forever with no clear payoff, I'd lose interest fast. I needed that quick feedback loop to stay motivated.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap to me. I'd look at them and think 'I should do this' but then the required stuff was already overwhelming me, so the optional things just added to my anxiety. At least with required activities there was external pressure pushing me. Optional stuff relied on self-motivation and honestly, I didn't have much of that left by midterm.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a bunch of lecture slides and PDFs early in the semester with good intentions. I'd study from those on my tablet when I didn't have internet or when I just couldn't face logging into the LMS again. I also used YouTube videos from other instructors when our course materials weren't clicking for me. None of that showed up anywhere.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours going through my downloaded notes, trying to understand concepts, making diagrams on paper. I'd be genuinely struggling with the material, working through problems by hand. But the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days. It probably looked like I'd given up when really I was just studying in a way that felt less overwhelming.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download video lectures and watch them multiple times offline, especially before exams. I'd also screenshot important slides and review them on my phone during commutes or breaks. The LMS only registered that initial download, not the hours I spent reviewing afterward. It was easier to study without the constant reminders of everything else I was behind on.
Q12: Around week six or seven, everything just hit me at once. I had seven online courses going simultaneously and the assignments started piling up from all of them. My anxiety spiked really hard and I kind of froze. I'd log in, see all the things I needed to do, feel completely paralyzed, and then log out without doing anything meaningful. It became this awful cycle.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload was crushing and I was constantly confused about priorities. When I was exhausted, I'd default to passive activities like watching videos or reading discussions instead of actually working on assignments. The fatigue made it impossible to concentrate on anything demanding. I was present on the LMS but not really productive.
Q14: All the time. I'd have the LMS open for hours, clicking through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a whole video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. I was going through the motions to feel less guilty about not studying, but I wasn't actually learning. It was like my body was there but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I avoided the assignments constantly even though I knew they were killing my grade. The thought of starting them triggered so much anxiety that I'd do literally anything else - watch more videos, participate in discussions, organize my notes - just to avoid facing them. It sounds stupid but starting felt impossible. I kept thinking I'd do it tomorrow and tomorrow never came.
Q16: Deadlines made me panic-login. I'd suddenly become super active on the LMS in the 24 hours before something was due, frantically trying to catch up on materials I should have studied weeks ago. But by then it was usually too late to actually complete the assignment properly. The deadline pressure didn't help me prepare better, it just made me more anxious and scattered.
Q17: I barely got any feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it was usually too late to matter for my understanding of the next topic. The lack of feedback made me feel invisible, like it didn't matter whether I engaged or not. After my CBT session, I tried to reframe this but honestly, the damage was kind of done by then.
Q18: The course structure was overwhelming with seven courses at once. There were so many modules and deadlines across everything that I couldn't keep track. I participated a lot in discussions and watched videos because those felt manageable, but the actual structured progression through assignments fell apart for me. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually made it easier to drift.
Q19: The visual elements and video content made engagement easier for me. But the sheer volume of required assignments and the way they built on each other made it harder. Once I fell behind on one assignment, everything after that felt impossible. There wasn't really a way to catch up or recover, which killed my motivation to even try.
Q20: That's literally me. I think it happens when you're engaged with the community and the content but completely paralyzed when it comes to producing work. The anxiety of submitting something imperfect or wrong can be worse than the anxiety of not submitting at all. You're trying, you're there, but something breaks down between consuming and creating.
Q21: I guess some people are just more efficient or better at studying offline. They don't need to be constantly logged in to understand the material. For me, high activity didn't translate to understanding because I was using the LMS as a comfort blanket rather than a learning tool. Quality matters more than quantity of logins.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, how long you spend, what you click on. But they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how much you were struggling emotionally. They can't capture the hours of offline study or the panic attacks before logging in. They show behavior but not the meaning behind it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with what you're learning, not just showing up. It's when the material matters to you and you're actively trying to understand it, not just going through motions. For me, I was engaged with the community and the idea of learning, but I wasn't engaged with the actual work of producing anything. That disconnect defined my whole experience.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually wanted to succeed or how hard I was fighting against my own anxiety. It wouldn't show the CBT session I did trying to get my head straight, or the hours I spent paralyzed staring at assignment prompts I couldn't start. It wouldn't show that my high engagement was partly a coping mechanism for feeling completely lost. The data tells a story but it's missing all the emotional context.
